content.text: library is a single mechanism that makes everything work: the
. Understanding it unlocks everything else about async programming — why
pauses a function, how multiple tasks run without threads, and why blocking code breaks everything.
The event loop is a loop that runs inside your Python process and manages the execution of all asynchronous tasks. It sits at the center of
and is responsible for deciding which piece of code runs at any given moment.

In simple terms, it is a program that keeps asking: "Is there any work ready to be done?" If yes, it does that work. If the work hits a point where it needs to wait — for a network response, a file read, a timer — it pauses that task, picks up another, and checks back later. It does all of this in a single thread, one step at a time, but because waiting time is shared efficiently, many tasks can make progress at once.

Most programs waste enormous amounts of time sitting idle. When your code sends a request to a database and waits for the answer, the CPU has nothing to do for potentially hundreds of milliseconds. Traditionally, the solution was threads — run many threads in parallel so the CPU is always doing something. But threads introduce complexity: race conditions, deadlocks, and heavy memory usage.
The event loop provides a different solution. Instead of running many threads, it runs one thread and schedules tasks cooperatively. A task voluntarily gives up control when it needs to wait (using
), and the event loop hands control to another ready task. This yields the benefits of concurrency — many things progressing at once — without the dangers and costs of threading.
: web servers, API clients, database-heavy applications, chat systems, and any program that spends most of its time waiting for external systems to respond.

Think of a restaurant kitchen with a single chef. Orders come in. The chef starts cooking a steak. While the steak is in the oven, the chef doesn't stand there waiting — they move to preparing the salad, then chop vegetables for the next order. Every time something needs time (the oven, the fryer, resting meat), the chef sets it aside and works on something else. When the oven timer goes off, the chef comes back to finish that dish.

The chef is the event loop. The dishes are coroutines. The oven, fryer, and timer are I/O operations. The key insight is that the chef only needs one pair of hands because all the actual waiting happens in the equipment, not in the chef.
This works beautifully as long as the chef never gets stuck doing something that takes a long time without being able to step away — like hand-grinding spices for ten minutes without stopping. In
, that is what blocking code does, and it is the most common mistake.
The event loop maintains several internal data structures:
holds tasks that are ready to run immediately. When the loop iterates, it picks tasks from this queue one at a time and runs them until each task yields control by reaching an

holds tasks that have been suspended because they are waiting for something — a network socket to become readable, a timer to expire, a file operation to complete. These tasks are registered with the operating system's I/O multiplexer (on most systems, this is
holds lightweight callbacks — simple functions to be called once, without suspension. These are scheduled via
On each iteration of the loop:
All currently ready callbacks and task steps are run until they yield.
The loop calls the OS's I/O polling mechanism (e.g.,
) with a timeout equal to the soonest scheduled timer. The OS returns a list of I/O events that are ready.
For each completed I/O event, the waiting task is moved back into the ready queue with its result.
Any timers that have expired are also fired, moving their tasks or callbacks into the ready queue.
This is what makes it a "loop" — it cycles continuously through these steps until there is nothing left to do.
Architecture — the loop cycle in detail
The diagram above shows the top-level lifecycle. Below is a more detailed look at what happens within a single iteration.
Each coroutine is wrapped in a
is what drives a coroutine forward. When you write:
keyword causes the coroutine to suspend and register that Future with the event loop. The loop records that "this task is waiting for this Future to complete" and moves on.

One second later, the event loop's timer fires. It marks the Future as done, which causes the Task to be moved back into the ready queue. On the next iteration, the task resumes from exactly where it left off — after the
), the flow is similar but involves the operating system:
The coroutine registers a file descriptor (the socket) with the event loop.
The event loop registers that descriptor with the OS using
The OS notifies the event loop when the socket has data.
The event loop wakes the waiting task and feeds it the data.

. Only one piece of Python code runs at any given moment. Tasks do not truly run simultaneously — they take turns. This means you do not need locks or mutexes to protect shared data from concurrent modification, as long as you stay within
. No two tasks will ever run at exactly the same moment.
This is fundamentally different from threading, where truly parallel execution can happen and data races are real. In
points, which makes reasoning about concurrency much simpler.
Starting and accessing the event loop
In modern Python (3.10+), you almost never create or manage the event loop directly. The standard entry point is

import asyncio

async def main():
    print("Hello from a coroutine")
    await asyncio.sleep(1)
    print("Done waiting")

asyncio.run(main())
Runs the given coroutine until it completes.
Shuts down the loop and cleans up.
If you are inside a running coroutine and need a reference to the loop, you can get it with:
But in most real programs, you do not need to touch the loop directly. Higher-level APIs like
How coroutines interact with the loop
A coroutine only runs inside a task. When you call a coroutine function (a function defined with
), you get back a coroutine object — not a result. The coroutine does not start executing until it is either:
ed from another coroutine (which runs it but ties up the caller), or

import asyncio

async def fetch_data(source):
    print(f"Starting fetch from {source}")
    await asyncio.sleep(2)          # Simulates an I/O wait
    print(f"Finished fetch from {source}")
    return f"data from {source}"

async def main():
    # Create two tasks — they run concurrently
    task1 = asyncio.create_task(fetch_data("API"))
    task2 = asyncio.create_task(fetch_data("Database"))

    # Wait for both to finish
    result1, result2 = await asyncio.gather(task1, task2)
    print(result1, result2)

asyncio.run(main())
Both tasks start immediately and run concurrently. While
is sleeping (simulating I/O), the event loop runs
. The total time is approximately 2 seconds, not 4 — both sleeps overlap.
What "blocking" means and why it kills the loop
Because the event loop is a single thread, anything that occupies that thread without yielding will freeze all other tasks. This is called
A function blocks the loop if it:
Performs synchronous file or network I/O (like
Does a long CPU-intensive computation (like processing a large dataset)

import time
import asyncio

async def bad_task():
    time.sleep(3)        # Blocks the ENTIRE loop for 3 seconds

async def good_task():
    await asyncio.sleep(3)   # Yields control; loop runs other tasks
, every other task in the loop is frozen. This is the most critical concept for beginners to internalize: the loop can only do other work during
For blocking CPU work, the solution is
, which offloads the work to a thread pool:

import asyncio

def heavy_computation(n):
    return sum(i * i for i in range(n))

async def main():
    loop = asyncio.get_event_loop()
    result = await loop.run_in_executor(None, heavy_computation, 10_000_000)
    print(result)

asyncio.run(main())

Consider an HTTP server that needs to handle 1,000 simultaneous requests, each of which queries a database. With threads, this would require 1,000 threads — each consuming memory and needing OS scheduling overhead.
, a single-threaded server handles all 1,000 requests through one event loop. Each request's coroutine runs until it hits
, then suspends. The event loop handles the I/O, and when the database responds, the coroutine resumes. The total memory footprint is a fraction of the threaded approach.
FastAPI and aiohttp are popular Python web frameworks built on this principle. A FastAPI endpoint defined with
runs inside the event loop. During a database query like
, the thread is free to handle other incoming requests.
are all synchronous. Calling them inside a coroutine blocks the event loop. Use
returns a coroutine object and does nothing. Python will warn you about this with a
In Jupyter notebooks, the event loop is already running. Calling
A loop that computes a hash or processes images directly will starve other tasks. Always offload with
when you want tasks to run independently without blocking the caller. Use
when you want to launch multiple tasks and wait for all of them. Set task names with
to avoid tasks that hang indefinitely waiting for slow I/O:
The asyncio event loop can handle tens of thousands of concurrent connections on a single thread when the workload is I/O-bound. Benchmarks for async Python web servers like
(which runs FastAPI) show performance competitive with Go and Node.js for I/O-heavy workloads.
CPU-bound work: no amount of async design helps if each task needs significant computation. For this, combine
The GIL: Python's Global Interpreter Lock means even with threads, true parallel CPU execution is blocked.
sidesteps this by not competing for CPU in parallel — only one thing runs at a time intentionally.
For maximum throughput on a multi-core machine, run multiple
processes (one per core) behind a reverse proxy like Nginx, or use
out of the box. The broader ecosystem includes:
— async HTTP client (drop-in async replacement for
— drop-in replacement for the default event loop, implemented in Cython on top of
The event loop is a single-threaded scheduler that runs coroutines by cycling through ready tasks, suspending them at
points, and resuming them when their I/O completes.
Tasks only yield control voluntarily — at
Blocking the loop — via synchronous I/O,
, or CPU-heavy code — freezes all other tasks. It is the most important pitfall to avoid.
is the standard entry point; it creates the loop, runs your top-level coroutine, and shuts everything down.
to wait for multiple tasks at once.
to offload to a thread or process pool without blocking the loop.
The event loop model excels at I/O-bound concurrency and can handle thousands of simultaneous connections on a single thread with low memory overhead.
